question: What is the difference between a type predicate (is) and an assertion function (asserts)?

options.text: Type predicate returns boolean and is used in if, assertion throws an error or narrows the type after the call
Type predicate only works with primitives, assertion — with objects
options.explanation: Both mechanisms work with any types — primitives and objects. The difference is in usage pattern (if vs throw)

options.text: Assertion functions are faster because they don't return a value
options.explanation: The difference isn't in performance but in semantics: predicate is a check (is?), assertion is a guarantee (must be!)

options.text: Type predicate narrows the type only in the true branch, assertion narrows in both branches
options.explanation: Assertion doesn't have 'branches' — after a successful call the type is narrowed, on error an exception is thrown

question: Why doesn't TypeScript narrow the type when using Array.filter without a type predicate?

options.text: filter takes a callback with boolean return type, and TypeScript cannot infer narrowing from an arbitrary boolean expression
filter always returns an array of the same type by specification
options.explanation: TypeScript has a filter overload with type predicate that returns an array of the narrowed type. But an explicit predicate is needed

options.text: This is a TypeScript bug that will be fixed in future versions
options.explanation: This isn't a bug — TypeScript correctly handles type predicates. Without an explicit predicate, the compiler cannot guarantee narrowing

options.text: filter only works with primitive types
options.explanation: filter works with any types. Narrowing depends on the type predicate in the callback, not on the element type

question: What happens if a type predicate promises a type but the runtime check is incomplete?

options.text: TypeScript will trust the predicate and narrow the type, but a runtime error may occur due to the mismatch
TypeScript will produce a compilation error
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't verify the correctness of runtime checks inside a type guard — it trusts the return type annotation

options.text: The program won't compile
options.explanation: TypeScript fully trusts the type predicate. The responsibility for check correctness lies with the developer

options.text: TypeScript will automatically add the missing checks
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't modify runtime code. A type predicate is the developer's promise to the compiler

question: Which of the following statements about assertion functions are true?

options.text: An assertion function must either throw an error or return void
After a successful assertion function call, TypeScript narrows the variable's type
Assertion functions cannot be async
Assertion functions can return boolean instead of void
options.explanation: asserts functions always return void. If you need boolean — use a type predicate (is)

options.text: Assertion functions only work with the unknown type
options.explanation: Assertion functions work with any types: unknown, union types, generic parameters, etc.

question: How do you create a generic type guard that checks for a property on an unknown object?

options.text: function hasProperty<K extends string>(obj: unknown, key: K): obj is Record<K, unknown> with check typeof obj === 'object' && obj !== null && key in obj
function hasProperty(obj: unknown, key: string): boolean with check obj[key] !== undefined
options.explanation: Without a type predicate (is), the function doesn't narrow the type. Also obj[key] causes a TypeScript error since unknown can't be indexed

options.text: function hasProperty<K>(obj: Record<K, unknown>, key: K): boolean
options.explanation: This variant doesn't accept unknown — it already requires Record, which defeats the purpose of checking unknown data

options.text: function hasProperty(obj: object, key: string): obj is { [key]: unknown }
options.explanation: Computed properties can't be used in a type predicate this way. You need Record<K, unknown> with generic K
